figures.caption: Figure 1. Comparison between existing ZSSAR methods and our proposed SkeletonContext. Conventional ZSSAR methods directly align skeleton features with textual descriptions, but the absence of contextual cues creates a semantic gap that hinders discrimination between similar actions. In contrast, SkeletonContext reconstructs language-driven contextual semantics (e.g., objects) and injects them into skeleton representations, enabling finegrained, context-aware zero-shot action recognition.

Table 1. Comparison of generalized zero-shot action recognition accuracy on the NTU RGB+D datasets. SkeletonContext achieves competitive or superior harmonic-mean (H) performance across most evaluation splits, indicating balanced generalization from seen (S) to unseen (U) classes.

Table 2. Comparison of zero-shot learning (ZSL) accuracy on the NTU RGB+D datasets. SkeletonContext achieves strong or comparable results across multiple splits, demonstrating effective generalization to unseen action categories.

Figure 2. Overview of SkeletonContext. The model enriches skeleton features via Cross-Modal Context Prompt Module (Sec 3.3) and Key-Part Decoupling Module (Sec 3.4) for context-aware zero-shot action recognition. Both modules are guided by LLM–derived contextual knowledge (Sec 3.2) during training, enabling semantic grounding and cross-modal alignment.
